- **References:** `<wR9Ej.21285$Er2.3997@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fkb2u352j37hb6p3qlpdv1s7a9phkkskts@4ax.com> <2ae2u3pdmt6dq1uct0khm06a6scrqhtqll@4ax.com> <13u30pf8roat4c8@corp.supernews.com> <81b4d762-1150-4f29-8a50-38df9eba2d07@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <b3o7u35ok0ta6bgm21qa44bffin6b855i5@4ax.com> <gd9au3l60edgomp67ajpchd5ddq6iij5s7@4ax.com> <06cfu31im7u9pmeptn292gi942c2l01gfq@4ax.com> <830gu315ogqb2h4eh45mbami4a2pcbjegp@4ax.com> <f6ufu3d01p4lgr0bl61m7uklp4coj110vh@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 24 Mar 2008 13:00:13 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Mon, 24 Mar 2008 13:35:43 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>beneficiary to be my infirmed brother or mother.  

If you die and were providing more than half your monther's support, she is eligible for
'survivor benefits. 

>>Someone counted more than 1,000 federal programs that use marital status.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>partners).

Those are civil contracts between cohabitants. I was talking about government handouts and
other privileges granted only to spouses. 

>>A candidate advocating that wouldn't get many female votes. 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>benefit of my social security, instead of the old wife?    

She doesn't get any of your benefits while you're alive. After you die, your unmarried  ex
AND current wife are entitled to survivor benefits when they hit retirement age (65-67).
They are entitled at any age if they have kids under 17.

Oddly, their survivor benefits can be higher than yours were.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: McCain's religion was Re: Eliot Spitzer, antismoking leader, self-destructs

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-03-25T07:42:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<330iu3l8vmo7ipi5152lm2i77luqidg7ch@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fkb2u352j37hb6p3qlpdv1s7a9phkkskts@4ax.com> <2ae2u3pdmt6dq1uct0khm06a6scrqhtqll@4ax.com> <13u30pf8roat4c8@corp.supernews.com> <81b4d762-1150-4f29-8a50-38df9eba2d07@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <b3o7u35ok0ta6bgm21qa44bffin6b855i5@4ax.com> <gd9au3l60edgomp67ajpchd5ddq6iij5s7@4ax.com> <06cfu31im7u9pmeptn292gi942c2l01gfq@4ax.com> <830gu315ogqb2h4eh45mbami4a2pcbjegp@4ax.com> <f6ufu3d01p4lgr0bl61m7uklp4coj110vh@4ax.com> <96rgu3lr84d02v66ankaqurv2t0taf956j@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 24 Mar 2008 21:28:10 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>>Or allow for other partnerships - let me declare my social security
>>beneficiary to be my infirmed brother or mother.  
>
>If you die and were providing more than half your monther's support, she is eligible for
>'survivor benefits. 

Good.   But she doesn't get my "spousal" benefit while I'm alive even
though I'm buying just as much social security insurance as the
married guy next door.

>>>Someone counted more than 1,000 federal programs that use marital status.
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>other privileges granted only to spouses. 

I don't believe there should be such hand-outs based upon marital
status - base them on need.    My example of supporting my mother or
my brother should get the same consideration as my supporting my wife.

>>>A candidate advocating that wouldn't get many female votes. 
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Oddly, their survivor benefits can be higher than yours were.

But why shouldn't she be able to get my insurance benefit while I'm
alive?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: McCain's religion was Re: Eliot Spitzer, antismoking leader, self-destructs

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-03-25T12:40:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<anfiu3p483021r6iksir50v2d29hu80ghg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2ae2u3pdmt6dq1uct0khm06a6scrqhtqll@4ax.com> <13u30pf8roat4c8@corp.supernews.com> <81b4d762-1150-4f29-8a50-38df9eba2d07@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <b3o7u35ok0ta6bgm21qa44bffin6b855i5@4ax.com> <gd9au3l60edgomp67ajpchd5ddq6iij5s7@4ax.com> <06cfu31im7u9pmeptn292gi942c2l01gfq@4ax.com> <830gu315ogqb2h4eh45mbami4a2pcbjegp@4ax.com> <f6ufu3d01p4lgr0bl61m7uklp4coj110vh@4ax.com> <96rgu3lr84d02v66ankaqurv2t0taf956j@4ax.com> <330iu3l8vmo7ipi5152lm2i77luqidg7ch@4ax.com>`

```
On Tue, 25 Mar 2008 07:42:55 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Mon, 24 Mar 2008 21:28:10 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>married guy next door.

There is no spousal benefit while you're alive. YOU get a retirement benefit based on YOUR
(not joint) earnings. If she's retired, she gets her own retirement benefit. You get the
same as the married guy next door.

>>>>Someone counted more than 1,000 federal programs that use marital status.
>>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>my brother should get the same consideration as my supporting my wife.

That's welfare, not insurance. Your 401K, life and health insurance don't pay out based on
need. Social Security was sold to the people as an insurance program, not welfare.
Generally, states administer welfare.

>>>>A candidate advocating that wouldn't get many female votes. 
>>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>alive?

Because it was set up during the 1930s, when the model was big strong man breadwinner
taking care of family while alive. 

The biggest gap in the system is medical insurance. If you're over 65 & retired, YOU are
covered by Medicare. Your under 65 wife is not. Her only options are private insurance and
getting it through work. Your Social Security retirement benefit isn't enough to pay her
private insurance primium, typically $1,000/mo. If she can't work, the choices are move to
another country or get divorced and she goes on welfare.
```

```
On Thu, 20 Mar 2008 11:32:01 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On Wed, 19 Mar 2008 16:07:28 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
><judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>history.   

While I agree with tim's earlier post on the incompetence except I was
dubious about the invasion, I am currently of the following belief.

1.  It is far harder to leave an area of conflict than to enter it.

2.  The UN has had peacekeeping troops in Cyprus for over 20 years.

3.  The US had troops in Japan and Germany for decades after World War
II.

4.  We finally have a general who seems to understand
counter-insurgency and is handling it better.

5.  I think torture is couner-productive even though I suspect the US
has used it most wars (there were stories out of Viet Nam).

6.  There are a number of people in Iraq who have bet their lives that
the US will not precipitously pull out.

7.  Those who criticize the Iraqi's for corruption and slow progress
have not watched our Congress in action (pun intended).  Note the slur
is bi-partisan.

Clark Morris
```

```
On Thu, 20 Mar 2008 20:17:33 -0300, Clark F Morris
<cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:

>On Thu, 20 Mar 2008 11:32:01 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
>wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Very True.

>2.  The UN has had peacekeeping troops in Cyprus for over 20 years.
>

Make that more than 30 years.  I lived in Nicosia, Cyprus for a year.
All they are doing is keeping long time cultural enemies Turkey and
Greece from using their Cypriot counterparts from tearing each other
apart.  Until Turkey and Greece become "bosom buddies" this division
will remain.


>3.  The US had troops in Japan and Germany for decades after World War
>II.
>

They still have troops there and in Okinawa (a Japanese island).
Totally unnecessary in my view but there they are.

>4.  We finally have a general who seems to understand
>counter-insurgency and is handling it better.
>

Are you referring to Petraeus?  For whatever reasons, he was able to
convince Gates and Bush that more troops were needed, hence the surge
which has been successful.  The troop level that is in Iraq now should
have been the troop level in March of 2003.  He's probably the first
General they've had in command that Bush actually listens to and
believes a political solution must accompany a military solution and
that the Iraq police and soldiers must take on more of the burden.


>5.  I think torture is couner-productive even though I suspect the US
>has used it most wars (there were stories out of Viet Nam).
>

There was torture used in Viet Nam by both sides, however I don't
recall it becoming as public and as much of a propaganda tool for the
other side to use during that time.  Abu Ghraib just fueled the
radical Muslims fire and gave them something else to use in their
propaganda against the west.  Gitmo isn't helping our cause either.

>6.  There are a number of people in Iraq who have bet their lives that
>the US will not precipitously pull out.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Clark Morris

Regards,

         (o o)

copulate me."

Steve
```

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:hPRDj.21794$r76.7423@bignews8.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> existence, no?

Not necessarily.

IMPOSING Democracy on anyone is simply...undemocratic.

Some countries may not work well under Democracy. It is not a perfect form 
of Government, simply better than the alternatives. For Democracy to work 
you need responsible, thinking citizens who can separate Church and State.

If a country is living in the Middle Ages, riddled with superstition and 
fanatical religious zeal, it may well be simply UNABLE to adopt a workable 
democracy. Religious states are not Democracies; they are subject to the 
dictates of God, as perceived by the population at large. The more extreme 
that perception, the less room for Democracy. The God of most major 
religions is not known for being democratic; one reason people adopt 
religion in the first place is because they want leadership.

You don't think for one minute that, if everyone in the USA (or even the 
vast majority) held the beliefs you do, it would be a Democracy, do you? :-)

(Me, and people like me would be first against the wall...hardly a 
democratic process...)

The answer is education, not coercion.

And some respect for the rights of others to be what they want to be 
(provided they do no harm) would not be out of place, either.

But, until then, "simply bombing the terrorists" may be a good course of 
action... (provided you can definitively identify "the terrorists" of 
course...)

Pete.
```

```
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> terrorists" of course...)

I'm not saying it would be easy. It may not even be possible. Look at
how much trouble Russia is having, and they chose democracy themselves.
But world terrorism presents a desperate situation, requiring desperate
measures. This is not a school picnic here. Many people in civilized
countries are spoiled into thinking that every problem can be solved
neatly. Some problems do not have solutions, and others have only messy,
expensive and difficult solutions ones. This is not a problem that will
just go away if we just ignore it.

As you say, I'm all for killing the terrorists, when they can be found.
But part of the "education" process is to provide a social environment
in which that can take place. As Bush pointed out, democracies are not
prone to starting wars with innocent neighbors. I do not think it is
the population of Iraq that is so opposed to democracy; the problem is
being imported from other countries, like Iran.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> weapons of mass destruction, we change our excuse.

Anyone who thinks Sadam Hussein wasn't interested in WMD is ignorant
of recent history and clueless about human nature. If we did not find
them, it was because he had moved them elsewhere, and/or not yet
developed what he wanted. What would have been better, to have waged
WWII in Europe, or taken Hitler out when he first started violating
the Armistice agreement? The latter would have saved some 50 million
lives and the devastation of Europe. Sadam Hussein, like Joseph Stalin,
is one of the few men in history who have fair claim to being more evil
than Hitler. Even Hitler had friends and people he cared about, but
Saddam Hussein, like Stalin, had no such human emotion and was more
like a reptile than a human. Hussein was a serious danger to anyone on
the planet, and I for one will gladly pay my share of taxes to take him
out. But people clueless about human nature, like Neville Chamberlain,
never understand true evil until it bites their heads off, and then it
is too late, unfortunately, usually for many others.
```

- **References:** `<15jqt3litm05kmehmnq3jbhr3rna5pjq40@4ax.com> <13tr5f61o1a716c@corp.supernews.com> <1e4tt3lc4dftu6b5j0t82hjhjf52ogecpu@4ax.com> <578tt3t3co30m5jkfgmd4h5b09mlb5ral5@4ax.com> <13tvn3697c4hb33@corp.supernews.com> <1hnvt3t3uic6u3fhoarpmv748ta9gna4bk@4ax.com> <hPRDj.21794$r76.7423@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <64b0miF2ah9mfU1@mid.individual.net> <NQYDj.22013$r76.18528@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <nu72u3tfho6o0ln3mgnpj1h08sep9vqcc0@4ax.com> <P6aEj.21293$Er2.15390@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Wed, 19 Mar 2008 10:16:04 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>is too late, unfortunately, usually for many others.

But that's not the point.  Bush SAID before the UN and the American
people one of the reason's for invading Iraq was because Sadam HAD
WMD's.  The fact is, and there is almost no doubt that Bush knew this,
he hadn't had any for nearly 10 years having disposed of them after
the Gulf War.  Bush also said before the UN and the American people
that Sadam was linked to Al Qaeda.  Again, something he knew not to be
true, but was used as another reason to invade Iraq. And how about the
way he humiliated Colen Powell with the neuclear Milk Factory or
nuclear train to nowhere or the attempt (untrue) to buy radioactive
material from Niger?  Lies, upon lies, upon lies. 

Just because Sadam was "interested" in WMDs is no reason to invade.
Fidel Castro has been "interested" in WMDs for over 30 years and Bush
didn't invade him.  Hell, Hugo Chavez is probably interested in WMDs
and we're not invading him.  

You compare Sadam to Stalin (not even close) as what?  A reason to
assuage your guilt for supporting Bush and his invasion of Iraq?  If
being inhuman to your people is the criteria, then why haven't we
bombed China for their treatment of Tibet?  Why haven't we invaded
Burma for their suppression of democracy in that country?  Or invaded
North Korea whose people are slowly starving and have a demigod for a
ruler? Why haven't we put a substantial force in Darfur to help end
the genocide there?  Bottom line is, your support for Bush and his
Iraq invasion is illogical.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"I wan' all dem kids to do what I do, to look up to me. I wan' all the kids to
copulate me."
--  ex-Chicago Cubs outfielder Andre Dawson on being a role model
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Eliot Spitzer, antismoking leader, self-destructs

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-03-19T13:22:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KRcEj.21483$Er2.16748@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<15jqt3litm05kmehmnq3jbhr3rna5pjq40@4ax.com> <13tr5f61o1a716c@corp.supernews.com> <1e4tt3lc4dftu6b5j0t82hjhjf52ogecpu@4ax.com> <578tt3t3co30m5jkfgmd4h5b09mlb5ral5@4ax.com> <13tvn3697c4hb33@corp.supernews.com> <1hnvt3t3uic6u3fhoarpmv748ta9gna4bk@4ax.com> <hPRDj.21794$r76.7423@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <64b0miF2ah9mfU1@mid.individual.net> <NQYDj.22013$r76.18528@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <nu72u3tfho6o0ln3mgnpj1h08sep9vqcc0@4ax.com> <P6aEj.21293$Er2.15390@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <9nf2u3h0hk9l9kjhugok70efv3mjjq91l5@4ax.com>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> and we're not invading him.

Ahem. Saddam actually *used* WMD on the Kurds, people in his
own country. I think that demonstrates far more than mere *interest*.

> You compare Sadam to Stalin (not even close) as what?  A reason to
> assuage your guilt for supporting Bush and his invasion of Iraq?  If
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Iraq invasion is illogical.

I'm not saying Saddam Hussein was equal to Hitler or Stalin in the
amount of damage he did, but in the evil of nature. Given the same
opportunities, Saddam would have been just as evil and ruthless as
either of them.

Saddam was in a position to capture the oil fields of Saudi Arabia.
You think 9/11 was damaging to the U.S.? That would be *nothing*
compared to the damage caused to all industrialized countries if he
had done so. Look what he did in Kuwait. I would prefer to have troops
stationed in Iraq to prevent that from happening, than to have troops
stationed in Saudi Arabia *after* it happened. Iraq would certainly
have been nuked in such an eventuality. Jimmy Carter made that
official U.S. policy. Anyone think that would have been better than
this?

I don't know how honest or dishonest George Bush was about Iraq. But
I know with certainty that I am very glad Saddam Hussein is gone, and
I think we are better off today than we would have been had we not
invaded Iraq, whatever his reasons were. I also know that it's easy
to criticize in hindsight, and much harder to know what's the best
strategy beforehand. It's also easy to *say* worse things would not
have happened, when it isn't your neck on the line having to make the
decisions. George Bush's primary responsibility is to protect American
lives and property, and that applies to *probable* threats as well as
formally proven and documented threats. Intelligence is a complex and
difficult business; you almost *never* know anything with certainty.

Your objections remind me of the people harping that Y2K turned out
not to be so bad. Well, only idiots expected their cars and microwaves
to explode. But the reason why Y2K wasn't bad was because millions of
programmers worked their brains out fixing the problems in time. I was
one of them, and damaged my health in the process. It's easy to harp
and complain, after the problem has been fixed. Terrorism isn't fixed,
but Saddam Hussein is fixed, permanently, and terrorism has been held
back, at least to a degree. As George Bush said from the beginning, we
may not live to see the end of this war with terrorism. Our children,
and maybe even our grandchildren, may be fighting it. Some problems
are just not easily or quickly solved; that's life.

Just hope and pray that the liberals don't get their way and pull the
U.S. out of Iraq too soon. I can think of nothing that would encourage
and embolden the terrorists more. Regardless of whether or not the Iraq
war was a good idea, pulling out too early would be a disaster, and the
stupidest thing we could possibly do, at this point.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Eliot Spitzer, antismoking leader, self-destructs

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2008-03-19T13:30:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e6461239-a54c-482d-befa-ba606af04cdc@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<15jqt3litm05kmehmnq3jbhr3rna5pjq40@4ax.com> <13tr5f61o1a716c@corp.supernews.com> <1e4tt3lc4dftu6b5j0t82hjhjf52ogecpu@4ax.com> <578tt3t3co30m5jkfgmd4h5b09mlb5ral5@4ax.com> <13tvn3697c4hb33@corp.supernews.com> <1hnvt3t3uic6u3fhoarpmv748ta9gna4bk@4ax.com> <hPRDj.21794$r76.7423@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <64b0miF2ah9mfU1@mid.individual.net> <NQYDj.22013$r76.18528@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <nu72u3tfho6o0ln3mgnpj1h08sep9vqcc0@4ax.com> <P6aEj.21293$Er2.15390@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <9nf2u3h0hk9l9kjhugok70efv3mjjq91l5@4ax.com> <KRcEj.21483$Er2.16748@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
On 19 Mar, 18:22, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>  George Bush's primary responsibility is to protect American
> lives and property, and that applies to *probable* threats as well as
> formally proven and documented threats. Intelligence is a complex and
> difficult business; you almost *never* know anything with certainty.
>

There is no doubt that Saddam Hussein had developed and possessed
weapons of mass destruction (WMD being chemical, biological and
nuclear weapons systems and in Saddams case both chemical and
biological weapons had been developed) and the means to deliver them
(rockets and artillery shells).

It was said that these weapons could be made ready at 45 minutes
notice and that they were a threat to the west (a nebulous statement
which permits any threat to Israel or our military forces stationed in
the Gulf to be interpreted as a direct threat to the UK).

I am not aware of any statement that he had nuclear weaponry nor that
he was able to deliver any weapon to targets outside of the middle
east.

However, some people (the media as I recall) took the view that the
threat to the UK was by nuclear weapons delivered by (in development
but not proven) ballistic missiles. Such interpretations were
unfounded in fact.

Further, any such interpretation was not denied by the government of
the UK despite their knowing that it was not true and that the
intelligence upon which it had been based was from one unreliable
source, was unsubstantiated, was contradicted by known reliable
intelligence and was probably falsehoods provided by a former refugee
from Saddam's justice.

The approach to justifying the war was botched by the UK government
and by the US government. In reality they did not need the WMD
argument to support them and, as we now know for certainty, beyond
some aging examples of chemical and biological weapons the programs
for WMDs did not exist.

What was wrong with using the humanitarian argument to remove Saddam?
Sanctions were not working and all it would have taken was just one
infraction of the peace treaty from the first Gulf war to start the
second.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:KRcEj.21483$Er2.16748@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>> the genocide there?

Excellent questions. It is ridicuolous to claim the moral high ground in one 
area, and not in all. You're either moral and good or you're not.

Being moral when it suits you is not being moral.


>> Bottom line is, your support for Bush and his
>> Iraq invasion is illogical.

Not so much illogical as simply political. Logic and politics seldom mix 
well.
>
> I'm not saying Saddam Hussein was equal to Hitler or Stalin in the
> amount of damage he did, but in the evil of nature. Given the same
> opportunities, Saddam would have been just as evil and ruthless as
> either of them.

Since when was being ruthless an indication of evil? When OUR generals do it 
is "stregth of purpose" and "tenacity", when the opposition do it , they are 
"ruthess". In neither case is it an indication of "evil".

And what are the criteria for measuring "evil of nature"?

Is it the degree to which someone disagrees with us?

I don't believe people are "evil of nature". I reckon people are basically 
good. Shit happens to them or they get corrupted by greed or power or 
whatever, and that makes them behave badly. Saddam was no better or worse a 
person than anybody else. Neither was Hitler, Attila the Hun, Stalin, Saint 
Paul or Saint Francis of Assisi.

Evil is in the eye of the beholder.

As long as you advocate bombing and killing people ("terrorists") you are 
morally no better than those you target. If they are "evil" so are you.

(Note: I'm not saying we shouldn't strike back at terrorists; just that we 
shouldn't delude ourselves that we have any more moral right to do so than 
they had to attack us... two wrongs don't make a right; they just make us 
feel better and hopefully prevent further wrongs by eliminating the 
perpetrators...)

>
> Saddam was in a position to capture the oil fields of Saudi Arabia.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> invaded Iraq, whatever his reasons were.

Do you think the majority of people in Iraq think they are better off? Does 
it matter to you?

As you watch the price of gas at the pump rise unstoppably, do you think all 
the dead young men (on all sides) were worth the sacrifice?

What difference has it made?

Getting Saddam out of Kuwait was a proper thing to do, nothing to do with 
gasoline (although I suspect that was certainly a contributing factor to the 
decision to get involved). Invading Iraq, on the other hand, was NOT a moral 
thing to do, WMD or no WMD. As has been pointed out by others here, there 
are many "rogue states" who DO actually have WMD. The West does not invade 
them.

Saddam treated people who opposed him, ruthlessly and badly. He needed to be 
removed, but whether that should have been done by interference from the 
West or not, is arguable.

> I also know that it's easy
> to criticize in hindsight, and much harder to know what's the best
> strategy beforehand.

Yes, it is. That's why some time for reflection and looking at possible 
outcomes is usually a good thing before sending in the Marines.

> It's also easy to *say* worse things would not
> have happened, when it isn't your neck on the line having to make the
> decisions.


It is easy to SAY anything. That's why politicians do so...

> George Bush's primary responsibility is to protect American
> lives and property, and that applies to *probable* threats as well as
> formally proven and documented threats.

Putting young men in harm's way is not a good way to protect their lives or 
property. If people are to die, and sometimes they have to, it should be a 
VERY carefully weighed and calculated strategy that minimises the risk to 
them, and everyone, from the Commander in Chief to the troops on the ground 
should realise what the plan is, what the possible outcomes are, and why it 
is necessary to do what is being done. In other words, there should be a 
clear entrance strategy, a clear plan of action, and a clear exit strategy.


> Intelligence is a complex and
> difficult business; you almost *never* know anything with certainty.

So "intelligence" is a misnomer for it then, isn't it?

>
> Your objections remind me of the people harping that Y2K turned out
> not to be so bad. Well, only idiots expected their cars and microwaves
> to explode.

Did anybody really believe that, or are you simply repeating an urban myth 
whipped up by journalists?

And, if some people DID believe it, does that mean they are "idiots"? What 
about people who are simply not well informed on technology?

I believe you are far too free with labelling people as idiots or morons 
simply because they disagree with you, but then, I would say that :-)

>But the reason why Y2K wasn't bad was because millions of
> programmers worked their brains out fixing the problems in time. I was
> one of them, and damaged my health in the process.

Working oneself to the point of health endangerment is hardly very bright; 
some might think it idiotic...:-)

Yes, a huge amount of valuable work was done, and credit is due to those who 
did it. But let's not forget that the computer industry caused the problem 
in the first place; it was therefore beholden on the computer industry to 
fix it.


> It's easy to harp
> and complain, after the problem has been fixed. Terrorism isn't fixed,
> but Saddam Hussein is fixed, permanently, and terrorism has been held
> back, at least to a degree.

Has it? The "terrorism" is inspired by perceived injustice and inequity, 
further fuelled by religious extremism. Until these things are addressed, 
the "War on Terrorism" cannot be won, and will, indeed, last for 
generations. The weapons that are needed are ability to listen and negotiate 
on the one hand, and education on the other. Instead the usual redneck 
solution of overwhelming force has been adopted by a redneck President (with 
his own agenda) who is far from the sharpest tool in the box, and whose 
actions should even embarrass other rednecks.

>As George Bush said from the beginning, we
> may not live to see the end of this war with terrorism.

What "beginning" was that? He didn't say it in his speech after 9/11 (which 
I read, and found quite inspirational...)

I'm picking he arrived at this conclusion after several years of watching 
the military"solution" fail...

> Our children,
> and maybe even our grandchildren, may be fighting it. Some problems
> are just not easily or quickly solved; that's life.

NO problem is easily or quickly solved if the solution involves overwhelming 
power. The problem with such solutions is that they do not address the root 
cause of the problem. (they can temporarily make the problem appear to go 
away, but trouble is simmering just under the surface and re-emergence is 
inevitable. Where people have grievances they need justice. At the very 
least they need to be listened to and respected.)

>
> Just hope and pray that the liberals don't get their way and pull the
> U.S. out of Iraq too soon.

Hoping and praying may be poor substitutes for action. Perhaps voting (and 
persuading other s to do so too...) might be a more positive way to address 
the problem...

> I can think of nothing that would encourage
> and embolden the terrorists more.

The "terrorists" don't need encouraging or emboldenment and pulling troops 
out of Iraq will neither increase nor diminish their resolve.

(It will definitely save American lives and probably save Iraqui lives, 
though...)

These are people fanatically committed to a "cause", prepared to destroy 
themselves and others, their motivation is hardly subject for dispute.

When Bin Laden is finally captured, someone else will take his place (he's 
probably already training a number of replacements), and that replacement 
probably won't be over six feet tall and need dialysis. Given that it has 
proved impossible so far to capture or kill Bin Laden (who doesn't exactly 
blend into the scenery), there is even less hope of catching his successor. 
The best chance is to infiltrate his organization and that doesn't seem to 
be so easy.

(You might ask yourself why it isn't easy... given that his organisation is 
"evil", you'd think there would be people queuing up to bring it down... 
Could it be that the adherents to it DON'T see it as "evil"?.... Gosh, maybe 
"evil" is a subjective concept as I mentioned above...)

The War on Terrorism is NOT about personalities and military might. Perhaps 
a change of US president may afford an opportunity to re-evaluate more 
carefully.

> Regardless of whether or not the Iraq
> war was a good idea, pulling out too early would be a disaster, and the
> stupidest thing we could possibly do, at this point.

I disagree, and I think an increasing number of Americans disagree too. The 
Iraquis should resolve their own problems, and should be left to do so. If 
that means they install a regime unfriendly to the West, well, them's the 
breaks... The problem is, and has always been, about oil...

However, a regime that was hostile to the West would STILL sell oil on the 
world market, because it is in their interest to do so. So the idea of this 
source drying up is an improbable one.

The only difference would be that Bush and his cronies would lose control of 
it.

Pete.
```

```
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
> opposition do it , they are "ruthess". In neither case is it an indication of "evil".

It's not just about "how" but "why". Saddam's "why" was all

> And what are the criteria for measuring "evil of nature"?

If you can't answer that yourself, then there is no explaination that

about motive and character.

> Is it the degree to which someone disagrees with us?

Hardly. :-)

> I don't believe people are "evil of nature".

Sorry. Then you are clueless.

> Evil is in the eye of the beholder.

Only if there is no such thing as evil, and you only see it as
situational ethics. Again, that's clueless.

> As long as you advocate bombing and killing people ("terrorists") you are morally no better than those you target. If they are 
> "evil" so are you.

So, you equate killing a small child for fun with executing a
Charles Manson with killing entirely by accident? See, you
are clueless. It isn't just the killing, it's also the reason for
the killing. Reason or motivation has to do with character.

> (Note: I'm not saying we shouldn't strike back at terrorists; just that we shouldn't delude ourselves that we have any more moral 
> right to do so than they had to attack us... two wrongs don't make a right; they just make us feel better and hopefully prevent 
> further wrongs by eliminating the perpetrators...)

Why should we "strike back" at terrorists, if killing is, by
definition, bad? If there is no moral absolute, then there
is no good or bad. Sorry, but that's clueless.

>> Saddam was in a position to capture the oil fields of Saudi Arabia.
>> You think 9/11 was damaging to the U.S.? That would be *nothing*
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do you think the majority of people in Iraq think they are better off? Does it matter to you?

Do the majority of the people in Russia think they are better off?
Neither society society has the experience with freedom to know.
In a sense, they're like babies in democracy.

> As you watch the price of gas at the pump rise unstoppably, do you think all the dead young men (on all sides) were worth the 
> sacrifice?
>
> What difference has it made?

No one can answer that. Rarely in history is there certainty of
result beforehand. Leaders must make decisions in a fog of
uncertainty. Did Roosevelt know that the Japanese were about
to attack Pearl Harbor? The fact is that the U.S. had information
to that effect. It's also a fact that the information was only part
of the whole picture which, for Roosevelt at the time, was far
from clear. This is a fact that few of severe critics of the Iraq
war take into consideration. It's easy to be a Monday Morning
Quarterback once the result is known. It's a far different thing
to make the decisions beforehand.

> Getting Saddam out of Kuwait was a proper thing to do, nothing to do with gasoline (although I suspect that was certainly a 
> contributing factor to the decision to get involved). Invading Iraq, on the other hand, was NOT a moral thing to do, WMD or no 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> exit strategy.

What do you insist that Bush did not carefully weigh those things?
I'm sure he did. Presidents don't make decisions all alone, they have
a multitude of counselors.

>> Intelligence is a complex and
>> difficult business; you almost *never* know anything with certainty.
>
> So "intelligence" is a misnomer for it then, isn't it?

That's lame. :-) As you know, I'm talking about information "intelligence"
as from the CIA, NSA, etc.

>> Your objections remind me of the people harping that Y2K turned out
>> not to be so bad. Well, only idiots expected their cars and microwaves
>> to explode.
>
> Did anybody really believe that, or are you simply repeating an urban myth whipped up by journalists?

I don't know how many people actually belived it, I just remember
people spouting such nonsense. I do remember being asked by
people about such things.

> And, if some people DID believe it, does that mean they are "idiots"? What about people who are simply not well informed on 
> technology?

Anyone who uses an automobile and never has to enter a date
into it should realize they a correct date isn't necessary for an
automobile to function. Was there ever a story (before Y2K)
circulated where someone's car stopped and the fdate had to
be entered for it to run? I seriously doubt it. Many people do
not know how to set the date on their VCRs (this is a common
cliche, the blinking 00:00), and they never exploded. You don't
have to be a techie to figure such things out.

> I believe you are far too free with labelling people as idiots or morons simply because they disagree with you, but then, I would 
> say that :-)

You're pretty free with labelling youself, like "liar". :-) It's not about
disagreeing with anything, it's about having no understanding.

>>But the reason why Y2K wasn't bad was because millions of
>> programmers worked their brains out fixing the problems in time. I was
>> one of them, and damaged my health in the process.
>
> Working oneself to the point of health endangerment is hardly very bright; some might think it idiotic...:-)

I agree, it was stupid. I am a person who has always takem my
responsibilities very seriously. I knew many people were depending
on my work, and "in the heat of battle" allowed that to drive me too
hard. I would not do it again.

> Yes, a huge amount of valuable work was done, and credit is due to those who did it. But let's not forget that the computer 
> industry caused the problem in the first place; it was therefore beholden on the computer industry to fix it.

I agree, but management should have started fixing the problem
sooner, rather than waiting until the last minute.

>> It's easy to harp
>> and complain, after the problem has been fixed. Terrorism isn't fixed,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> whose actions should even embarrass other rednecks.

Hogwash! You can't eliminate the "perceived" you can only deal
with realities. When people choose to perceive themselves as
eternal victims, what is there to do about it? Force their society
to stop teaching hate to their children, as the Moslem cultures do?
Israel, for example, would dearly love to stop all hostilities with
the Arabs, but they can't, because the Arabs want to destroy Israel,
and there's nothing else that will satisfy them. There is no solution
for such a situation, other than for Israel to entirely destroy the
Arabs or submit to annihilation.

>>As George Bush said from the beginning, we
>> may not live to see the end of this war with terrorism.
>
> What "beginning" was that? He didn't say it in his speech after 9/11 (which I read, and found quite inspirational...)

I taped his speach before congress, and yes, he did say that, quite clearly.

>> Our children,
>> and maybe even our grandchildren, may be fighting it. Some problems
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> to be listened to and respected.)

As I said above, that's hogwash. Those people *want* to hate.
How are the Moslem people being "persecuted" considering
they are the vast majority within most of the geographic locations
they inhabit? The West has been pouring money in there for
decades to buy oil. But look at the difference in contributions to
society between the vast Moslem population and tiny Israel.
Those from tiny Israel *dwarf* by many times, the contributions
form the entire Moslem population. If they would start teaching
their children more math and science and stop teaching hate, that
might one day be different. How do you suggest that be done
from the outside? Do you not realize they would literally have to
be conquered and enslaved to do it, unless something like Bush
is trying to do by establishing democrary in Afghanistan and
Iraq right now doesn't work? Do you not see that Bush is trying
right now to do exactly what you suggest? Do you have a better
idea than just criticism? If so, then please trot it out. Please give
us your detailed and infallible plan that your comments imply
must exist.

>> Just hope and pray that the liberals don't get their way and pull the
>> U.S. out of Iraq too soon.
>
> Hoping and praying may be poor substitutes for action. Perhaps voting (and persuading other s to do so too...) might be a more 
> positive way to address the problem...

I do intend to vote, nut what do you consider my post to be, if not
an attempt to persuade? :-)

>> I can think of nothing that would encourage
>> and embolden the terrorists more.
>
> The "terrorists" don't need encouraging or emboldenment and pulling troops out of Iraq will neither increase nor diminish their 
> resolve.

Again, you show cluelessness about human nature. People, even
terrorists, do not live in a vacuum; they are influenced by events.

> (It will definitely save American lives and probably save Iraqui lives, though...)
>
> These are people fanatically committed to a "cause", prepared to destroy themselves and others, their motivation is hardly subject 
> for dispute.

You ignore the inevitable human response to long term failure and
frustration and discouragement from unattained goals. Not all people,
which includes not all terrorists, respond identically. But people are
people, and have generally common emotions and responses to them.
It won't stop all terrorists, no. But effectively blunting their efforts
over time, while improving the lot of the population from which the
terrorists come (e.g. establishing democracy there, if that's possible)
will definitely have a positive effect over time. These things don't
come about over the space of a few years, it takes decades.

> When Bin Laden is finally captured, someone else will take his place (he's probably already training a number of replacements), 
> and that replacement probably won't be over six feet tall and need dialysis. Given that it has proved impossible so far to capture 
> or kill Bin Laden (who doesn't exactly blend into the scenery), there is even less hope of catching his successor. The best chance 
> is to infiltrate his organization and that doesn't seem to be so easy.

Not done yet does not equate to impossible. All it takes is one person
in the know about his whereabouts to decide to cash in on $50 million
U.S. dollars. Killing or imprisioning bin Laden will mean more for
Americans than for anyone else, because it will represent justice done.
It took a long time, but the U.S. finally got all the terrorists responsible
for the bombing of Pan Am flight 103, and there was far less motivation
there than in this.

> (You might ask yourself why it isn't easy... given that his organisation is "evil", you'd think there would be people queuing up 
> to bring it down... Could it be that the adherents to it DON'T see it as "evil"?.... Gosh, maybe "evil" is a subjective concept as 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> re-evaluate more carefully.

You keep criticising, but do not offer any alternatives. If you're so
clever, then lay out your detailed inevitably successful plans for us. :-)

>> Regardless of whether or not the Iraq
>> war was a good idea, pulling out too early would be a disaster, and the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> So the idea of this source drying up is an improbable one.

You talk about how unswervingly motivated to our destruction the
terrorists are, willing to face death itself, then blithely assume *any
regime* they brought into power would sell us oil. Clueless. :-)
```

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-03-21T09:05:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f9j7u3hm4ss0pm5oeh6f8rdm1g343edfm1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<nu72u3tfho6o0ln3mgnpj1h08sep9vqcc0@4ax.com> <P6aEj.21293$Er2.15390@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <9nf2u3h0hk9l9kjhugok70efv3mjjq91l5@4ax.com> <KRcEj.21483$Er2.16748@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <64dps5F2b04rcU1@mid.individual.net> <VJsEj.22063$Er2.509@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <le85u39gbc8ap69c9ue22rejt3v0av817r@4ax.com> <1wxEj.23857$dT.11519@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <7vd5u3dekseqq3hdlsv0mq1a0is0t3f646@4ax.com> <96edf2da-ac0e-45c0-9172-ef7d66575c09@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Fri, 21 Mar 2008 06:19:05 -0700 (PDT), Alistair
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>> So with these three choices - An Islamic terrorists, a Patriotic
>> soldier, and a wacko psychotic - are caught and brought to justice -
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>terrorist acts each year but there is no war on bathtubs.

In this subthread about intent, I suppose it is whether a bathtub
maker is more liable for intentional slippery tubs than for
unintentional slippery tubs.   Seeing how the tobacco industry was
finally brought down, I suspect so.

We like to punish who we think are bad more than others who do the
same thing but who we think are good.   That's about punishment, not
about safety.

The book _Freakonomics: A Rogue Economist Explores the Hidden Side of
Everything_, by Steven D. Levitt & Stephen J. Dubner is quite fun. One
stat that he brings forth is that your kid is in significantly greater
danger if you live next to someone with a swimming pool than if you
live next to someone with a gun.    

Some of this is helplessness - we get real upset when we read about
some Mad Cow meat getting through - while having much more dangerous
food practices at home.
```

```
further than others it is because he stood on the shoulders of giants.

predecessors. Those predecessors include pagan Greeks, pagan Hindus
and muslim Arab mathematicians. The western world owes the muslim
world a lot. We were ignorant and had denied science in favour of a
creationist nonsense at a time when they had universities and were
extending the boundaries of mathematics and astronomy (amongst other
sciences).
```

- **References:** `<13tvn3697c4hb33@corp.supernews.com> <1hnvt3t3uic6u3fhoarpmv748ta9gna4bk@4ax.com> <hPRDj.21794$r76.7423@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <64b0miF2ah9mfU1@mid.individual.net> <NQYDj.22013$r76.18528@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <nu72u3tfho6o0ln3mgnpj1h08sep9vqcc0@4ax.com> <P6aEj.21293$Er2.15390@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <9nf2u3h0hk9l9kjhugok70efv3mjjq91l5@4ax.com> <KRcEj.21483$Er2.16748@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <64dps5F2b04rcU1@mid.individual.net> <VJsEj.22063$Er2.509@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <bp85u31v22gnvl5v7t3a8av1v4eq11mpsd@4ax.com> <GIxEj.23859$dT.14946@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <b693f7c0-6788-45e4-ab3b-ca39267b0982@d4g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <rePEj.22198$Er2.9216@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Fri, 21 Mar 2008 09:03:07 -0500, Judson McClendon wrote:

> Recent Eastern cultures have not been conducive to innovation and
> invention. Even as intelligent and industrious as the Japanese are,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ahead of anything in the west. It's clearly not genetic, but cultural.

Have you never used a transistor radio, or a Sony Walkman?

The total quality movement, while it used ideas from Edwards Deming, was
really a Japanese thing. This has been one of the most important
innovations of the past few decades.

There is a western preoccupation with "who invented it". It is
usually more important, and more difficult, to take an idea and turn it
into something useful. The first programmable elctronic computer was built
by a German, and the British were ahead of the US in early computer
technology.

This was not what created the computer revolution though. Americans turned
these ideas into usable products.

Having said all that, the whole Confucian ethic, with its excessive
reverence for age and authority, and the centralisation of power and money
in traditional Chinese society produced a terrible stagnation that lasted
for many hundreds of years. In the west, the renaissance and the
industrial revolution swept similar tendencies away.

Tim
```

```
"tim" <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:

Yes, and neither transisters, sound recording, computers nor micro-
computers were invented by Japanese. :-)

> The total quality movement, while it used ideas from Edwards Deming, was

While important, the Japanese make improvements, not innovations.
The transister was an innovation, the computer was an innovation.
Making them better isn't an innovation. Without the inventions made
by others, they would have nothing to improve, no? :-)

> There is a western preoccupation with "who invented it". It is
> usually more important, and more difficult, to take an idea and turn it
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> these ideas into usable products.

Even there, the Japanese lag behind. They simply make products
somewhat better and cheaper then others. That's a good thing,
don't get me wrong, but it isn't the prime benefit, which is the
invention and significant innovation on inventions.

> Having said all that, the whole Confucian ethic, with its excessive
> reverence for age and authority, and the centralisation of power and money
> in traditional Chinese society produced a terrible stagnation that lasted
> for many hundreds of years. In the west, the renaissance and the
> industrial revolution swept similar tendencies away.

I agree. These things are cultural. That's one thing that concerns me so
much about blacks in the U.S.; so much of their culture tends to drag
them down, and does not encourage young blacks to strive and excel
in the things that enrich a people. What a waste. :-(
```

```
On Fri, 21 Mar 2008 12:13:05 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

By quoting and passing it on you are sponsoring and supporting
stereotypes.  The Bible teaches we are all the same.  You are being
hypocritical.  Being critical of a person's actions is far, far
different than painting a whole ethnic group with the same paint
brush.  And I do know that Allah is the Arabic name for God.

It is a compound word from the article, 'al, and ilah, divinity, and
signifies "the god" par excellence. This form of the divine name is in
itself a sure proof that ilah was at one time an appellative, common
to all the local and tribal gods. Gradually, with the addition of the
article, it was restricted to one of them who took precedence of the
others; finally, with the triumph of monotheism, He was recognized as
the only true God.


>> While your facts are true, if it were not for the Muslims we wouldn't

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>condemned them?

did.

I think Jesus would have praised the Muslim religion and culture.
Their basic tenets are well aligned with Christianity.
```

```
On Thu, 20 Mar 2008 07:26:33 -0500, Judson McClendon wrote:

> What do you insist that Bush did not carefully weigh those things?
> I'm sure he did. Presidents don't make decisions all alone, they have
> a multitude of counselors.

Have a look at the Bay of Pigs case before you repeat these sorts of
assumptions.

It was very clear that GWB was set on invading Iraq. He also
has a track record of getting rid of those who don't do along with him.
The potential for group-think and for telling the boss what he wants to
hear is enormous. It takes a special person to avoid these temptations,
someone of strong moral fiber.

Whether GWB is such a person is left as an exercise for the reader...

http://dir.salon.com/story/news/feature/2004/09/16/tsurumi/index.html

Re Judson's comments about Monday morning quarterbacking: I supported the
Iraq invasion and unlike some others I have not rewritten my personal
history to pretend I did not. I supported it because I did not want an
evil megalomaniac controlling the world's oil (I am referring to Saddam
Hussein here!).

But like almost everyone, I did not expect the massive incompetence of
every aspect except the initial military campaign.

Here are some examples

0. The lies about WMDs.

1. The language used ("Crusade" etc).

2. The lack of any post-war plan.

3. The sacking of the entire army, immediately creating a huge group of
people angry at the US, with military training, and nothing useful to do.

4. The naive assumptions that a country like Iraq would embrace democracy
and would forget past deadly tribal conflicts.

5. The severe corruption involved in the outsourcing of war provisions. Up
to 10 layers of contractors, each skimming their take off the top. In many
cases, less than 5% of the money spent is actually applied to the 

6. The use of torture and degradation for little good effect but at great
cost in PR terms.

Tim
```

```
On Thu, 20 Mar 2008 20:47:09 -0000, tim <TimJ@internet.com> wrote:

>Re Judson's comments about Monday morning quarterbacking: I supported the
>Iraq invasion and unlike some others I have not rewritten my personal
>history to pretend I did not. I supported it because I did not want an
>evil megalomaniac controlling the world's oil (I am referring to Saddam
>Hussein here!).

I dislike the criticism of Clinton for initially supporting the war.
We didn't have time nor the means for everybody to determine all of
the facts and evaluate everything.   Their job was to trust that the
President was telling the truth, support him - and then evaluate that
trust with further information.   That's why we have a president.

>But like almost everyone, I did not expect the massive incompetence of
>every aspect except the initial military campaign.

Agreed.   Certainly they were not prepared for what they got after the
invasion was completed.
```

```
On Fri, 21 Mar 2008 07:58:24 -0600, Howard Brazee wrote:

> I dislike the criticism of Clinton for initially supporting the war.
> We didn't have time nor the means for everybody to determine all of
> the facts and evaluate everything.   Their job was to trust that the
> President was telling the truth, support him - and then evaluate that
> trust with further information.   That's why we have a president.

The constitution specifically gives the power to declare war to Congress.
That is for precisely the reason that it is not wise to delegate that
decision to one person. At the time the US was caught up in a hysteria
about terrorism. Congress failed to fulfil its responsibilities. 

There were a few exceptions. Some people have a cool head in a crisis - the
kinds of people you may want answering that phone call at 3am.

Tim
```

```
think about them makes them "good" or "bad". If you don't understand that, 
then it isn't me who is clueless.

>

There is no such thing as evil any more than there is good. (No God, no 
Devil.) They are concepts invented by Humans. That doesn't mean there is no 
right or wrong behaviour or no morality. Neither does it mean that events 
have no consequences for people, which may be perceived as "good" or "bad". 
Generally the more positive an outcome for people, the more "good" it is; 
and vice versa. But there is no intrinsic "goodness or badness" in the 
events which occur. The Universe simply doesn't care. It isn't me who is 
lacking clues here.

written speech and take my hat off to the person who wrote it...

>
>>> Our children,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> As I said above, that's hogwash.

Listen and respect is "hogwash"?

I suspect the hogs in your neck of the woods are in dire need of 
cleaning...:-) The materials needed are obviously in very short supply...

Of course, there is no need to resolve conflict if you simply apply 
overwhelming force. There is no need for equity or justice or respect if you 
simply stamp on disagreement... Trouble is, that used to work, when people 
were uneducated and prevented from having access to knowledge. In those days 
they could be controlled by superstition and prohibited from thinking for 
themselves. Dissent meant eternal damnation in Hell fire. But time has moved 
on, and there is something about those evil little Humans that stops them 
from just rolling over; it makes them lie down in front of tanks, it makes 
them make statements like: "Give me Liberty or give me Death.", "Better to 
die on our feet than live on our knees...", it makes them demand freedom, 
question their corrupt leaders, and it makes them  seek justice, not just 
for themselves, but for all Men.

The most powerful nation on Earth can no longer impose its will by force of 
arms, alone. And many of its citizens realise that and are glad to look for 
other ways. You could say America is growing up.

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

comes from...?

I'm all in favour of education in ANY society. I see it as the best chance 
to bring down fanatical religious extremism and for that reason alone, I'd 
advocate it. Get people actually thinking and maybe they'll see through the 
nonsense that has been inculcated in them since birth.

Sadly it isn't just Moslem extremists who preach a doctrine of hate. (Some 
people in the West actually believe that concepts like respect for other 
cultures are "hogwash"... I know... it's shocking....)


> How do you suggest that be done
> from the outside?

I see no need to do it from the outside, and I think it is presumptuous to 
even want to. There are already Iraqi people studying in Western 
Universities. Iraqi women, (just like their sisters elsewhere) are pretty 
fed up with being third class citizens and there are mutterings of change. 
It starts with an undercurrent, whispered conversations in secret places, 
ideas not encountered before that inspire people to the realization that 
there are OTHER ways... Give it 30 years or so. Why do you suppose that 
regimes like the Taliban and the current Iranian rulers are SO emphatically 
against education? Intellectuals and scholars are first against the wall... 
because they know that a thinking population is much harder to control.

>Do you not realize they would literally have to

for Democracy to work.

the US has troops in Iraq or anywhere else. It is a different kind of war, 
but that obviously hasn't filtered through to you yet.

This is a war of IDEOLOGY. The weapons and tactics are not tanks, planes, 
and guns.

But you have consistently demonstrated here that you're not good on 
ideology. One idea is enough for you. The One True Way... (And you call ME 
clueless... :-))

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

>

If this is how he is captured, I don't believe it will be done for money. If

> Killing or imprisioning bin Laden will mean more for

See how the prospect of justice excites and pleases you? Why do you think 
that any other culture would not be equally pleased and excited to see some?

> responsible
> for the bombing of Pan Am flight 103, and there was far less motivation

I don't think that even one victim of that crash was resurrected by the

don't delude myself it changes anything.

>
>> (You might ask yourself why it isn't easy... given that his organisation 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> You keep criticising, but do not offer any alternatives.

I'm NOT "criticising". As always, I call them like I see them. What I'm 
expressing here are some opinions and they lead me to ask questions which I 
think should be addressed. Many Americans are coming to the same conclusions 
and the coming elections promise to be very interesting.

> If you're so
> clever, then lay out your detailed inevitably successful plans for us. :-)
>
 My personal cleverness has no bearing on US foreign policy.

I do know something about resolving conflict (and have the certificates to 
prove it...:-) however, even then I don't always apply what I know.

If you seriously want my advice (and I'm pretty sure you don't...) I would 
suggest a phased withdrawal of troops from Iraq (within a pretty agressive 
time frame), combined with ALL interested Iraqi parties being encouraged to 
sit around a table and try to establish what they see as the future for 
their country. That might be a democratic model or it might be an Islamic 
state, or it might be something in between. However, WHATEVER THEY decide 
should be binding and there should be no foreign interference or veto. The 
only condition woud be that there would be no haven for Terrorism, and any 
such haven would be subject to foreign attack.

In other words, their decision should be respected by the rest of the world, 
even if it doesn't go the way we might like.

Foreign aid would not be dependent on what they decide. You either give them 
aid or you don't. Aid could be targeted to schools, hospitals, law and 
order, and free exchange of students.

If they are unable to reach a compromise amongst themselves and decide to go 
for Civil War, there should be immediate withdrawal of all foreign forces 
and just let them get on with it... The problem there is that it endangers 
the oil supply and that is what this has always been about. That's a 
scenario that would need to be worked out with them, if and when it arose 
(maybe UN forces protecting oil supplies, and the revenues being held in 
trust in foreign banks until cessation of hostilities.)

Negotiation should not be conducted at the point of a gun.

All of the above is over simple and represents general approaches and 
attitudes. The details would have to be worked out for each scenario that 
arose.


>>> Regardless of whether or not the Iraq
>>> war was a good idea, pulling out too early would be a disaster, and the
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

You blithely assume that any regime the Iraqis came up with for themselves 
would be backed by terrorists.  It wasn't in the past, why should it be now?

Pete.
```

```
>> How are the Moslem people being "persecuted" considering
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Teach the Arabs Math and Science, eh? Any idea where the word "Algebra" comes from...?

And we still use Arabic numerals: 0123456789. But in truth, the high
civilization was really in Persia (now Iran) which isn't Arab. :-)

> I'm all in favour of education in ANY society. I see it as the best chance to bring down fanatical religious extremism and for 
> that reason alone, I'd advocate it. Get people actually thinking and maybe they'll see through the nonsense that has been 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> respect for other cultures are "hogwash"... I know... it's shocking....)

I have no respect for cultures that denigrate women, teach hate, and
typically mutilate the genitals of young girls.

>> How do you suggest that be done
>> from the outside?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> against the wall... because they know that a thinking population is much harder to control.

So, how many World Trade Center disasters are you willing to tolerate
in that 30 years, eh? Can I assume that if you and your family are to be
killed by terrorists, then you still advocate waiting another 30 years?

>>Do you not realize they would literally have to
>> be conquered and enslaved to do it, unless something like Bush
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Conquer and enslave them so we can set them free, eh?

Duh! Did you actually read what I wrote before responding?

> When you finish slavishly worshipping at the altar of Democracy as the One True Way, you might actually do some travelling and see 
> if there is anything of value in other systems, or even give some thought as to what is required for Democracy to work.

The proof of their culture is the fruit, as it is for any culture. Their
"fruit" is hate, violence and oppression.

> This is a war of IDEOLOGY. The weapons and tactics are not tanks, planes, and guns.

And how do you propose changing their ideology, if not by installing
a free democracy there?

>> All it takes is one person
>> in the know about his whereabouts to decide to cash in on $50 million
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> now.

Not necessarily. The "right person" just hsn't learned his whereabouts yet.

>> It took a long time, but the U.S. finally got all the terrorists responsible
>> for the bombing of Pan Am flight 103, and there was far less motivation
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> were caught, but I don't delude myself it changes anything.

Victims of crime may not be reassured by aprehension and punishment
of the criminals either. But it is a necessary process for a civilized
culture.

>> If you're so
>> clever, then lay out your detailed inevitably successful plans for us. :-)
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> attack.

You just contradicted yourself:

A. "However, WHATEVER THEY decide should be binding and there should
be no foreign interference or veto."

B. "The only condition woud be that there would be no haven for Terrorism"

The fact is, the Iraq government is not yet strong enough to stand on its
own. Another fact is, as you just said yourself, there must be *some*
external oversight to make sure the process works and doesn't become
a danger to the rest of the world.

Believe me, I don't know a single American who would not like to see
U.S. troops out of Iraq as soon as possible. The American people do
not want to militarily occupy other countries. But we don't like to be
victims of terrorism, either.
```

- **References:** `<hPRDj.21794$r76.7423@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <64b0miF2ah9mfU1@mid.individual.net> <NQYDj.22013$r76.18528@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <nu72u3tfho6o0ln3mgnpj1h08sep9vqcc0@4ax.com> <P6aEj.21293$Er2.15390@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <9nf2u3h0hk9l9kjhugok70efv3mjjq91l5@4ax.com> <KRcEj.21483$Er2.16748@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <64dps5F2b04rcU1@mid.individual.net> <VJsEj.22063$Er2.509@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <64g8qpF2bt08cU1@mid.individual.net> <X0DEj.23901$dT.19269@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Thu, 20 Mar 2008 19:09:31 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:


>> And you don't think US policy in the region is motivated by self interest?
>
>There's nothing wrong with self interest. The problem is when people
>think only in terms of their interests, to the exclusion of everyone else.

The suicide bombers are not thinking only in terms of their interests.
They are fighting for what is Right, even when the cost is their own
lives.

That is a bigger problem.

...

>Apparently you don't understand that my question was to illustrate the
>fallacy in yours. Sometimes the *best* answer to a question is a question,
>assuming the person responding to the second question has enough
>understanding to realize the import. Apparently not, in this case. :-)

Sometimes it is.   Obviously not this time.   Maybe you should have
chosen to answer more plainly this time.

>> My two questions above therefore follow naturally. They are not rhetorical and I should have thought an upright moral character 
>> such as yourself, armed with the absolute authority of your belief system, could have answered without hesitation.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Once again, you miss the point.

He was making a new point and asking a question.    Maybe you should
answer, ask him what his point was - and ask him what he thought about
your point.

...

>Believe me, I don't know a single American who would not like to see
>U.S. troops out of Iraq as soon as possible. The American people do
>not want to militarily occupy other countries. But we don't like to be
>victims of terrorism, either.

The definition of "as soon as possible" varies so much as to be
meaningless.

Some people say we invaded Iraq out of humanitarian reasons - which
leads to ask why we didn't invade Rwanda instead, saving considerable
money and doing more good.

And while we don't want to be victims of terrorism - that doesn't mean
that we agree that a particular action taken in the name of
anti-terrorism succeeds.

I'm sure you can come up with a multitude of cases where the
government's response for one need or another has failed to accomplish
the goal - and often has made the problem worse.   We all can.

But if we agree that the feds can and do make problems worse when they
intend to fix them - we should put aside our wishes and biases and
evaluate whether the invasion of Iraq helped or hindered bin Laden's
goals, whether they helped or hindered our security, helped or
hindered the influence of our values world wide, helped or hindered
our liberty...   but since that is water under the bridge - use those
evaluations as a baseline to decide what our next action should be.
```

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:X0DEj.23901$dT.19269@bignews1.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>>>>>
Most of this response was so lame I see no point in commenting on it, so I 
just snipped it instead. Masochists can consult the thread if they want to 
see the crazy non-sequiturs, unsupported inferences, and plain stupidity 
that passes for sparkling repartee in your mind.

However, there are a few things which you ascribe to me that are so far off 
the beam I guess I SHOULD make SOME comment:
>
> No, let's do as you spparently want, let the world self-destruct

Having respect for the rights of others and not interfering in their lives, 
is NOT letting "the world self-destruct", even though you see it as 
"hogwash". Resolving differences by dialogue rather than force, may be the 
only chance we have to PREVENT the world from destructing, especially as 
more small states acquire nuclear weapons.

>while
> philosophizing and telling ourselves that "people are inherently good."

I defined what I meant by "good" but you conveniently ignored that and 
decided to just misquote what was actually said, out of context, and throw 
it in as part of NATO policy on Kosovo...  nice going.

This approach is every bit as justifiable, on the observable evidence, as 
your bleak alternative, based on a 3500 year old superstition, of people 
being born in sin and therefore inherently evil. As people tend to live up 
to the labels applied to them, the outcomes from my philosophy are 
infinitely better than the outcomes from yours...

> Europe tried that in the provinces of former Yugoslovia.

You know nothing about what happened in the former Yugoslavia. (I was there 
during some of it...). I can assure you it had nothing to do with my 
philosophy. There is a big difference between invading a state to protect 
your interests (like oil wells..), and invading a state because the 
legitimate government has requested your assistance to prevent a genocide. 
The shameful thing in that particular instance is that outside interference 
took so long to arrive. (If they'd had oil, it would've been much faster...)

>Worked well,
> didn't it?

Much better than the solution in Iraq is working. Life is returning to 
normal and it hasn't required long term occupation by foreign forces.

>
> You apparently do not realize the limitations of human understanding.
>

Perhaps...  But I believe I may have insight into the limitations of YOUR 
understanding...:-)

Pete.
```

```
On 20 Mar, 12:26, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> > And what are the criteria for measuring "evil of nature"?

Arrant nonsense. You were asked a simple question: define the criteria
by which you measure "evil of nature". You have set yourself up as an
arbiter of morality without deigning to define the criteria that you
apply to justify your statements. How can we know whether you are
qualified to pass your judgements upon others? Without definitions,
then one mans' freedom fighter (as OBL would view himself) becomes
another mans' terrorist (as you would view OBL). Recent research has
shown that those who are involved in these acts of terrorism view
themselves to be moral and just.

>

>

and good, then we can only conclude that only those who are right-
thinking moral christians and active members of your local church
denomination are deemed to be non-evil and that the rest of us are
evil in your eyes?

>

You've set yourself up as an arbiter of all that is moral and good.
You denounce others for holding a viewpoint that is at variance with
your own but is not inconsistent with the teachings of your holy book.
Just who do you seriously think you are?

And BTW, your comment is insulting and says much about you.

>
```

```
humility to realise that one person does not necessarily have access to
the whole truth and that other people's viewpoints may also be valid. It
is about letting other people live their lives and not arrogating to
oneself the right to decide on behalf of others what is right and wrong.

Tim
```

```
> (Note: I'm not saying we shouldn't strike back at terrorists; just that we

Assuming that you are striking back at terrorists and not just at
people with  a different religion, politics, approach to polygamy,
etc., then you have a moral duty to apprehend terrorists (who by their
nature and actions work to impose their minority view upon a peaceful
majority).

actions are those of terrorists.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

>

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

It will give the extremists grounds to believe that their warped views
have succeeded and that further action (the return of Spain to the
Moors and the re-occupation of eastern europe as far as Vienna)....

It is a shame that a country so willing to bomb it's way into a war is
so incapable of putting up with the inevitable consequences:
casualties. And so eager to divest itself of the consequent moral duty
to return Iraq to a stable nation state.
```

- **References:** `<578tt3t3co30m5jkfgmd4h5b09mlb5ral5@4ax.com> <13tvn3697c4hb33@corp.supernews.com> <1hnvt3t3uic6u3fhoarpmv748ta9gna4bk@4ax.com> <hPRDj.21794$r76.7423@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <64b0miF2ah9mfU1@mid.individual.net> <NQYDj.22013$r76.18528@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <nu72u3tfho6o0ln3mgnpj1h08sep9vqcc0@4ax.com> <P6aEj.21293$Er2.15390@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <9nf2u3h0hk9l9kjhugok70efv3mjjq91l5@4ax.com> <KRcEj.21483$Er2.16748@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Wed, 19 Mar 2008 13:22:39 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That was during the Gulf War (like I said). During the time that Bush
was firing up everyone about Sadam, he no longer had WMD's.  


>> You compare Sadam to Stalin (not even close) as what?  A reason to
>> assuage your guilt for supporting Bush and his invasion of Iraq?  If
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No kidding.  Yet his evilness was of no threat to the US.  He was
being contained by the British and US Air Forces with the enforcement
of the no fly zones.  Again I point out that there were and still are
rulers in the world at least as evil as Saddam and the US is not
invading those countries.

>Saddam was in a position to capture the oil fields of Saudi Arabia.
>You think 9/11 was damaging to the U.S.? That would be *nothing*
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No way Saddam invades Saudi Arabia.  First, the US has had bases there
since WW2.  Secondly, the Saudi Arabian Air Force is and was better
equipped and talented and would have wiped out the Iraq's in short
order.  Saddam tried lobbing some missals at Saudi Arabia during the
Gulf War.  That didn't get him very far.  Your grasping at straws with
that argument.


>I don't know how honest or dishonest George Bush was about Iraq. But
>I know with certainty that I am very glad Saddam Hussein is gone, and
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Probably threats yes, not implausible ones as Iraq was.


>Your objections remind me of the people harping that Y2K turned out
>not to be so bad. Well, only idiots expected their cars and microwaves
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And taking out Saddam is no cure for terrorism either.  Even capturing
Bin Laden won't fix that.  And thanks to George Bush, terrorism is
going to last a lot longer than it would have had he actually put into
effects policies to combat the reasons for terrorists rather than
taking the easy way and invading an alleged terrorist country.

Osama bin Laden's purpose for the 9/11 attack was (and is) to provoke
massive retaliation from the US against Islam. He hoped that our
retaliation would finally unite Islam against the US as a common
enemy, and that his vision for a fundamentalist pan-Islamic state
would sweep away all the more moderate governments in the region, as
well as movements toward a pluralistic culture.

The invasion of Iraq is not only a distraction from more important
goals, but a provocation that is making Osama bin Laden's dream come
true.

The purpose of the original attack was to provoke our retaliation,
which would affect primarily the ordinary Islamic people, destroying
what trust they had for the US, and making them more willing to give
aid and comfort to the terrorists among them. To a surprising extent,
we have fallen right into Osama bin Laden's trap. The future of the
world depends on finding our way out. 

It's tempting to think that a war against terrorism can be won by
killing all the terrorists. In the real world, this naive plan doesn't
work. A serious attempt to find and kill all the terrorists also
sweeps up many ordinary people, and some of them and their relatives
become new terrorists, creating more terrorists than were destroyed.
The harder the authorities pursue this strategy, the more it looks
like genocide, and the more effectively they recruit new terrorists.

But unfortunately this isn't the way George Bush, Dick Cheney, Donald
Rumsfeld and many, many others think.  

>Just hope and pray that the liberals don't get their way and pull the
>U.S. out of Iraq too soon. I can think of nothing that would encourage
>and embolden the terrorists more. Regardless of whether or not the Iraq
>war was a good idea, pulling out too early would be a disaster, and the
>stupidest thing we could possibly do, at this point.

Our leaving Iraq would not embolden the terrorists.  They gain more by
our being their where they can easily irritate us and make us do
things, like we've been doing, to feed their propaganda machine. What
should and will be done when the Democrats gain control will be to
divert the troops from Iraq to Afghanistan to finish what GWB didn't.
The real war aginst terrorism lies in that country, not Iraq.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"I wan' all dem kids to do what I do, to look up to me. I wan' all the kids to
copulate me."
--  ex-Chicago Cubs outfielder Andre Dawson on being a role model
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
> than Hitler. Even Hitler had friends and people he cared about,

Such as Eva Braun and his niece (who committed suicide after suffering
years of hitlers' domineering patronage).

> but
> Saddam Hussein, like Stalin, had no such human emotion

Both Saddam and Stalin cared about some people. Saddam cared about the
members of his extended family until certain members (son-in-laws) did
the dirty on him and fled Iraq> He murdered them after luring them
back to Iraq with promises that all was forgiven. He still loved his
two daughters, two sons and wife until the sons were killed and the
father hung.

and was more
> like a reptile than a human. Hussein was a serious danger to anyone on
> the planet, and I for one will gladly pay my share of taxes to take him
> out. But people clueless about human nature, like Neville Chamberlain,
> never understand true evil until it bites their heads off, and then it
> is too late, unfortunately, usually for many others.

Beware of criticising Neville Chamberlain. He knew that the UK was
weak and unfit to fight a war against Germany. By waving the piece of
paper and declaring peace in our time he bought the UK valuable time
to prepare for war.
```
